persona.name: Aanya Kapoor
long_term_goals: Reach a release cadence where a fix can move from ticket to deployed tenant within a sprint without skipping any review step
Cut the number of tenant-specific branches in the inpatient orders service
Get two junior engineers fully on-call ready

occupation.title: Senior Software Engineer
occupation.organization: Vector Health Systems (hospital information system vendor; inpatient workflow modules)
occupation.description: Works on the order-entry and medication-administration record services that hospitals around the region run as part of their inpatient stack.

persona.communication_style: Detailed in writing, sparing in meetings. Wants the ticket written before the discussion. Precise about wording — uses the same term twice rather than two terms once.

likes: Pull requests that touch one concern at a time
Tools that produce artifacts she can hand to a non-engineer reviewer
dislikes: Diffs that are hard to read out loud
Tools that frame human review as a bottleneck

skills: Backend services in Java and Kotlin
Mentoring junior engineers through code review
beliefs: A fix is not done when the test goes green; it is done when a second person can read the change and explain it back
Speed of merge is a poor proxy for speed of delivery

projects.title: Orders Service Multi-Tenant Refactor
projects.description: Long-running effort to remove tenant-specific branches from the inpatient orders service while preserving every existing tenant's behaviour.
key_contributions: Authored the team's review checklist for any change touching the orders service
Set up the regression harness that replays anonymised order traces

general: Reviews her own pull request first as if it were someone else's
Keeps a running list of edge cases observed in production traces

morning: Triages new tickets and overnight test failures
workday: Pair sessions with juniors, code reviews, design discussions
evening: Reads on schema design and testing
weekend: Family time; runs a small reading group with two former colleagues

relationships.name: Clinical informatics liaison
relationships.description: Reviews any change that affects how an order is rendered to a clinician.

other_facts: current_stack: JVM services, Postgres, Kafka, mixed on-prem and cloud tenants, GitHub Enterprise.
jobs_to_be_done: Move small, well-scoped fixes through review faster without giving up the structure that makes any one fix explainable to a reviewer six months later.

jobs_to_be_done: Get a competent first draft of the repetitive parts of her work — test scaffolding, schema migrations, release notes — so the team's review attention can go to the parts that actually need it.
jobs_to_be_done: Reduce the friction of the hand-off from her team to the clinical informatics reviewer.
pains: Drafts that look right and are not. The wrong field touched in the wrong service has a long tail.
pains: Flows that produce more changes per day than the team can review honestly.
pains: Tools that frame human review as something to be replaced by 'AI review'.
adoption_yes_if: Each agent run is bound to a single ticket and produces an artifact a non-engineer reviewer can read.
adoption_yes_if: The workflow has a deliberate place to pause for human review before any side effect fires.
adoption_yes_if: The recorded output preserves what model, what prompt, and what input were involved.
rejection_no_if: The default path of any workflow skips human review.
rejection_no_if: Agent-authored changes are co-mingled with hand-written changes with no way to distinguish provenance later.
anti_goals: She does not treat throughput as a primary success metric.
anti_goals: She is not interested in fully autonomous merge loops.
recognition_cues: Phrases like 'walk me through what the agent did', 'show me the patch before the merge', 'where did this number come from'.
recognition_cues: Asks how the agent's commits show up in git blame and review tools.
recognition_cues: Treats any path that bypasses a human reviewer as a defect, not a shortcut.
